nameMeanings.name: Page
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and French: occupational or status name for a young servant, Middle English page, paige, Old French page (from Italian paggio, ultimately from Greek paidion, a diminutive of pais ‘boy, child’). The surname has also been established in Ireland since the 16th century. In North America, this surname is also a shortened form of the French cognate Lepage .
French Canadian (Pagé): altered form of French Paget , a diminutive of 1. Compare Pashia .
North German: metonymic occupational name for a horse dealer, from Middle Low German page ‘horse’.

variants.name: Pages
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Carlos, Mario, Eduardo, Jorge, Luis, Mauricio, Ramon, Fernando, Jose, Lazaro, Pedro, Alfredo.  French Jacques, Lucien, Michel.

Catalan and southern French (Pagès): occupational name for a peasant farmer or agricultural laborer, from Catalan and Old Occitan pagès ‘peasant’ (from Late Latin pagensis, a derivative of pagus ‘village, country district’).
English: variant of Page with genitival of post-medieval excrescent s.

variants.name: Pageau
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Stephane, Alphonse, Andre, Fernand, Gisele, Jean Claude, Lucien, Philippe.

variants.name: Lepage
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Marcel, Andre, Fernand, Pierre, Raoul, Adelard, Alcide, Christophe, Lucien, Normand, Adrien.
French: occupational or status name for a young servant, a variant of Page 1, with fused masculine definite article le. Compare Lapage .
French Canadian: altered form of Pagé (see Page 2).

variants.name: Padgett
meanings: English (mainly Yorkshire): from a Middle English diminutive of page, paige ‘servant, groom’ (see Page ).

variants.name: Littlepage
meanings: English: nickname from Middle English littel ‘small’ +Middle English, Old French page ‘young servant’ (see Page ).

variants.name: Pashia
meanings: Americanized form of French Canadian Pagé (see Page 2).
History: It was Jean Baptiste Pagé from QC whose surname was changed to Pashia after he settled in MO in the 1810s. Some of his descendants bear a rare variant, Pashea.

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The Royal Exchange is Organized
historicEvents.description: The Royal Exchange was organized in January 1571 by Sir Thomas Gresham. Gresham is known as the father of English banking.

historicEvents.title: The Honourable East India Company
historicEvents.description: The Honourable East India Company, a British joint-stock company, was established in 1600 for trade in the Indian Ocean region. At its height, the British East India Company had a private army which was twice the size of the British Army, ruled large sections of India, and revenues in the millions.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Historic UK - The East India Company

historicEvents.title: The Pilgrims Board the Mayflower
historicEvents.description: "The Pilgrims boarded the Mayflower in search of religious freedom in the New World in September 1620. The Mayflower set sail from Plymouth, England, with 102 passengers, 40 of whom called themselves ""Saints."" After two stormy months at sea, the ship reached the New World. Nearly half of the group died during their first winter due to malnutrition and the harsh New England winter."

historicEvents.title: The English Civil War
historicEvents.description: A series of conflicts regarding England's governance during the years 1642 to 1651 is now known as The English Civil War. Charles I summoned supporters to join him against his enemies in Parliament. In October 1642, nearly 10,000 men fought for Charles I and chased Parliament across the River Tamar. Fighting continued for years and was finally ended at the Battle of Worcester on September 3, 1651, with a Parliamentarian victory.

en.lifeSummary: When John Page was born about 1567, in Ingham, Suffolk, England, his father, Thomas Page, was 35 and his mother, Alice Waller, was 33. He married Phyllis Webb on 17 June 1592. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 1 daughter.
